risk: Encode() silently truncates RedirectTargetIDs when len > MaxRedirectTargets(8) instead of returning an error; confirmed via manual repro that IDs beyond the 8th are dropped with no error signal. This is a foundational catalog-record codec that split/, mvcc/, btree/, wal/ all depend on per LLD; any future caller (e.g. split/ producing >8 redirect targets) would silently lose routing data on disk.
recommendation: Change Encode() signature to return an error (or panic) on overflow instead of truncating; add a regression test exercising RedirectTargetIDs > MaxRedirectTargets.

risk: RESOLVED: prior finding (run 004-verification) that Encode() silently truncated RedirectTargetIDs beyond MaxRedirectTargets is fixed. Encode() now returns ([]byte, error) and hard-errors on overflow, symmetric with Decode(). Verified via full source read, new boundary+overflow tests passing under -race, and a repo-wide grep confirming zero stale callers of the old single-return-value signature.
recommendation: none - closed

risk: InsertSlot reuse of a tombstoned slot tracks available capacity via that slot data length (slotDataLength), which is overwritten with the new, possibly smaller, length on every reuse. Capacity is monotonically non-increasing across shrink-reuse cycles even though the underlying contiguous tuple-region footprint from the original allocation is not actually released; there is no separate reserved-capacity field (the 8-byte slot header has an unused reserved uint16 that could hold one) and no compaction. Not a memory-safety bug and not currently exercised by any caller, but will cause progressive internal fragmentation once catalog records are updated in place (e.g. by a future mvcc/ or split/ write path that shrinks then grows the same logical record across multiple updates), silently reducing effective page capacity over time with no error surfaced until the page fills up sooner than expected.
recommendation: Track original reserved capacity per slot (e.g. use the unused reserved field in the slot header) so reuse offers the original footprint rather than the shrunk one, or explicitly document that reuse capacity is the most-recent data length. Add a regression test: insert N bytes, delete, reinsert M<N bytes, delete again, attempt reinsert of a size between M and N to pin down intended behavior before 1.1.3 (free-list page) or any update-in-place feature is built on top of this reuse mechanism.

risk: FreePage(pageID) does not check isUsed before clearing the bit, so double-freeing a page is a silent no-op rather than a surfaced error. A future caller in split/ or mvcc/ that erroneously calls FreePage twice on the same page (e.g. via a bug in reference counting) will not be caught here; combined with no internal locking (documented as out of scope), this is a latent correctness trap for later phases that build page reclamation logic on top of this free-list.
recommendation: Add an isUsed(pageID) guard to FreePage returning an error on double-free, plus a regression test double-freeing a page; do this before wiring split/mvcc callers into FreePage.

risk: Free-list capacity is hard-capped at bitmapCapacityBits (~32724 pages / ~128MB catalog) because the free-list is a single fixed-size bitmap page with no chaining to additional bitmap pages. Also, a crash between the file-extension WriteAt (AllocatePage) and the following persistBitmap WriteAt+Sync leaves the file physically extended but the on-disk highestAllocated stale; Open()'s consistency check then hard-errors on reopen ('catalog is corrupt') rather than silently misreporting free/used state -- safe (no silent corruption) but a full denial-of-open until the WAL/crash-recovery phase adds real re-derivation.
recommendation: Track both the capacity ceiling and the crash-window reopen-failure behavior as explicit inputs to the engine/wal/ crash-recovery design; consider a multi-page or extensible free-list representation before catalogs need to exceed ~32k pages.

risk: Two-file durability coupling: IDAllocator persists its fileID high-water-mark in a separate sidecar file (.idalloc) from catalog.dat, with no cross-validation between the sidecar's high-water-mark and the actual max FileID present in catalog.dat records. A lost/independently-restored/never-created sidecar sitting next to a non-fresh catalog.dat silently resets fileID allocation to 1, risking collisions with FileIDs already recorded in existing catalog records. Not a bug for 1.1.4's isolated-allocator scope (its own test spec only pairs the allocator with a sidecar it created itself), but is a real gap once catalog CRUD wiring lands.
recommendation: Before wiring IDAllocator into catalog record insert/delete, add a cross-check (e.g. on NewIDAllocator or at first CRUD use) that the sidecar's high-water-mark is >= the max FileID actually present in catalog.dat, and/or ensure the sidecar and catalog.dat are always backed up/restored/migrated as one atomic unit.

risk: fmMu (catalog.go) globally serializes FileManager.ReadPage/WritePage/AllocatePage calls across ALL fileIDs, including the WritePage fsync (the dominant per-op cost), defeating the striped-mutex design's 'unrelated files never contend' goal for the I/O path. Confirmed via ad hoc probe test. Any future subtask building on Catalog (mvcc, split, wal) that assumes true cross-fileID I/O concurrency will inherit this bottleneck.
recommended_fix: Scope synchronization to FileManager's own shared mutable state (highestAllocated, bitmap) instead of wrapping ReadPage/WritePage/AllocatePage's full bodies in one mutex.

summary: activeMu (pre-existing global lock in catalog.go's insert path) serializes every Put's insert-side ReadPage/WritePage I/O across ALL fileIDs regardless of stripe, partially undercutting the striped-lock 'unrelated files never contend' design goal. Not introduced by this fix round; flagged as residual architectural gap for future throughput work.

fmMu global-serialization finding from 014-verification is resolved: fmMu removed from catalog.go; FileManager now uses a narrow internal mu (bookkeeping only, verified never held during ReadPage/WritePage I/O) plus new pageStripes[256] in catalog.go guarding same-page read-modify-write sequences. Verified via full read of file.go/catalog.go and TestCatalogFileManagerNarrowLockDoesNotSerializeAcrossIO (deadlock-style test, not timing-flaky).

B+Tree node on-disk format (LeafNode/InternalNode, NodeSize=4096) has no reserved field for the version counter that docs/LLD/btree.md's optimistic-read concurrency design requires; adding it later will force a format migration of already-persisted index/name.idx files.
recommendation: Reserve a version-counter field/byte in the node header before subtask 1.2.2+ (lookup/insert) locks in consumers of this format, or explicitly plan for a breaking format bump when concurrency (crabbing/optimistic reads) is implemented.

summary: encodeKeys() writes uint16(len(k)) length prefixes with no explicit per-key length guard independent of the NodeSize>needed check; currently dead-code-safe only because NodeSize(4096) << 65535, an unasserted invariant.
recommendation: Add an explicit check/comment coupling NodeSize < 65536 to the length-prefix encoding, so a future NodeSize increase doesn't silently reintroduce truncation.

description: Prior round flagged missing version-counter header space for optimistic-concurrency reads required by docs/LLD/btree.md. Follow-up fix reserved offVersion (8 bytes) between keyCount and body, growing offBody from 3 to 11. Verified all offset math, decodeHeader length guard, and size-calc functions (leafEncodedSize/internalEncodedSize) correctly derive from offBody with no hardcoded literals -- no silent off-by-8 corruption found.
residual_risk: Low. Version field is reserved and round-tripped but not yet atomically bumped/checked (CAS logic explicitly deferred to a later concurrency-focused subtask, per docs/LLD/btree.md). Future subtasks implementing optimistic reads must wire actual version-check/retry logic against this now-reserved field; if that logic is skipped or added incorrectly, the on-disk format itself will not need to change again, but concurrent-read correctness will still need separate verification at that time.

summary: ReadNode/WriteNode defensive error paths (reserved node ID 0 guard, unrecognized type discriminator, wrong-length write) are not exercised by any test in lookup_test.go.
recommendation: Add explicit negative-path unit tests for these error branches before 1.2.3 (insert) or 1.2.4 (delete) start relying on them being validated.

summary: Lookup's internal-node descent (sort.Search over Keys) is only exercised against internal nodes with exactly one separator key in buildTestTree; no test covers Children[i] selection for 0<i<len(Keys) on a node with >=2 keys.
recommendation: Extend buildTestTree (or add a supplementary fixture) with a >=2-key internal node once 1.2.3's real insert lands, or add a small standalone fixture now.

summary: gh issue #2's acceptance test spec for 1.2.3 literally requires 'go test ./engine/btree/... -run TestInsertSplit', but no test function named or substring-matching TestInsertSplit exists in insert_test.go (actual names: TestInsertEmptyTree, TestInsertUpsert, TestInsertLeafSplit, TestInsertInternalSplit, TestInsertOutOfOrder). Running the literal spec'd command yields 'no tests to run' with exit 0, a silent false-pass risk for any CI/grader trusting the issue's literal test spec.
recommendation: Rename one existing split test to TestInsertSplit, or add a thin TestInsertSplit wrapper that t.Run()s the existing leaf/internal-split sub-cases, before closing out 1.2.3.

summary: NodeAllocator (insert.go) reintroduces the same sidecar-state-file-loss/ID-reuse residual risk previously accepted as non-blocking for engine/catalog/idalloc.go in 1.1.4: if the .nodealloc sidecar file is deleted while the main index file survives, the allocator restarts its high-water-mark from 0 and can reissue already-used node IDs on next Insert, silently corrupting the tree.
recommendation: Track alongside 1.1.4's equivalent open item; consider a durability/consistency check (e.g. persisting a recognizable marker inside the main index file itself, or cross-checking against the highest node ID actually present) before the persist/reload subtask (1.2.6) locks in the on-disk contract.

summary: chooseLeafSplit/chooseInternalSplit have untested boundary cases: a single pathologically oversized key can leave chooseLeafSplit unable to reduce mid below 1 (Encode() then fails loud rather than corrupting, but untested); chooseInternalSplit can select mid=0 on a 2-key overflowing internal node, yielding a non-root 0-key/1-child internal node that node.go's doc comment implies is root-only.
recommendation: Add a regression test with a few very long keys forcing an uneven split, and assert/document whether a non-root 0-key internal node is an accepted invariant relaxation or should be guarded against.

summary: repairEmptyLeaf (engine/btree/delete.go) discards store.ReadNode's isLeaf return in all four sibling-inspection call sites (left/right borrow, left/right merge), treating any same-parent sibling of an emptied leaf as a LeafNode unconditionally. When a legitimate grandparent-splice repair leaves a bare leaf as a direct sibling of internal nodes under one parent, a subsequent leaf-merge fallback silently splices the internal sibling's entire live subtree out of the parent's Keys/Children, causing permanent, silent data loss (reproduced: insertN(40000) then sequential delete fails at i=15525, losing an internal subtree of ~161 keys / ~170 children never actually deleted). Not caught by any shipped test because no test in this diff (or 1.2.3's) builds a tree past 2 levels deep (~30000+ sequential keys needed given ~169-per-node fanout at NodeSize=4096).
recommendation: Fix repairEmptyLeaf to check isLeaf before treating a sibling as a LeafNode (skip/guard the internal-sibling case); add a depth-appropriate test (real 3-level tree via Insert, or a test-only reduced-fanout fixture) asserting the grandparent-splice/internal-repair path is genuinely exercised, before subtask 1.2.4 is marked complete.

summary: repairEmptyLeaf's new tombstone-fallback (neither same-parent sibling is a leaf, post-grandparent-splice mixed-type shape) permanently tombstones the empty leaf with no reclamation path within 1.2.4's own code -- a narrower, strictly safer variant of the already-accepted tombstone/abandoned-node-ID gap, but a new distinct trigger condition worth tracking explicitly for 1.2.5/1.2.6 design discussions.
recommendation: When persist/reload and any deeper rebalancing strategy are designed in 1.2.5/1.2.6, explicitly include this mixed-type-sibling tombstone shape in the discussion.

summary: No test directly and narrowly targets repairEmptyLeaf's new neither-sibling-usable fallback branch in isolation (e.g. a hand-constructed small tree confirming the fallback is reached and that subsequent inserts/deletes against the tombstoned leaf still behave correctly). The shipped 40k-key regression test proves the critical data-loss bug is fixed but doesn't isolate this branch specifically.
recommendation: Optional: add a smaller, targeted unit test that deterministically forces the neither-sibling-is-a-leaf shape and asserts correct subsequent behavior.

summary: PrefixScan against a genuinely empty tree (rootNodeID == reservedNodeID) is not covered by any test in scan_test.go. By code inspection, PrefixScan surfaces the same reserved-node-ID error Lookup already surfaces in this situation (descendToLeaf -> store.ReadNode(reservedNodeID) -> explicit guard error), consistent with lookup.go's own documented out-of-scope precedent (lookup_test.go likewise has no EmptyTree case) -- not a new regression, just an inherited, still-untested gap.
recommendation: When 1.2.6 or a later hardening pass addresses tree lifecycle from empty state, add an explicit EmptyTree case to both lookup_test.go and scan_test.go asserting this error-surfacing behavior is intentional.

summary: PrefixScan implements plain string-prefix matching (strings.HasPrefix), not path-segment-aware subtree matching: a prefix like 'auth' also matches an unrelated topic path 'authorize/grant' that merely shares leading bytes, not the same '/'-delimited subtree. This is deliberate and tested (TestPrefixScanPrefixIsCompleteKey asserts 'authorize/grant' IS included for prefix 'auth'), consistent with issue #2's literal 'string prefix' spec, but callers (e.g. the ingestion-agent shortlisting consumer named in docs/LLD/btree.md) must pass a trailing path separator (e.g. 'auth/') to get true subtree semantics rather than byte-prefix semantics.
recommendation: Add a one-line callout to PrefixScan's doc comment in scan.go clarifying that callers wanting subtree semantics must include the trailing path separator in prefix.

summary: docs/LLD/btree.md remains scaffold-level (still 'Status: scaffold only') and does not document the .root sidecar, SaveRoot/LoadRoot, or the caller-owns-commit-timing design, despite persist.go's own doc comment pointing to it for full rationale. Staleness carried through every subtask of task 1.2, not newly introduced here.
recommendation: Now that task 1.2 (B+Tree) is fully complete, do a dedicated LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/btree.md before starting task 1.3.

summary: No test exercises a corrupted/wrong-size .root sidecar file (e.g. truncated by a crash mid-write before Sync completed). LoadRoot defensively returns an explicit error for this case (size check) but this path is unverified by any test.
recommendation: Add a follow-up test writing a truncated .root file and asserting LoadRoot returns a non-nil error.

summary: No test verifies writes made after the last SaveRoot call are correctly absent/stale upon reload -- current test only checks pre-SaveRoot writes ARE visible after reload, not the inverse.
recommendation: Add a follow-up test: SaveRoot, then Insert more keys without a second SaveRoot, close/reopen, and assert the additional keys are absent.

summary: OpenWriter's resume path does not validate the resumed segment's tail for a torn/partial record before appending further writes; a restart after a mid-write crash (before 1.3.4/1.3.5 land) would blindly O_APPEND new records after torn bytes, producing a segment ReadSegment can no longer parse past the torn point. Explicitly documented as deferred to 1.3.4/1.3.5 in the doc comment and commit message, not a silent oversight, but a real interim gap.
recommendation: When implementing 1.3.4 (recovery replay) and 1.3.5 (crash-injection), ensure OpenWriter's resume path itself is updated to validate/tolerate a torn tail on the resumed segment before allowing further Appends, not only via a separate explicit recovery invocation.

summary: writer.go's doc comments and the commit message claim the durability discipline matches engine/catalog's 'WriteAt+Sync' idiom, but Append actually uses plain sequential file.Write+Sync (no WriteAt call) -- a reasonable choice for a genuinely append-only log, but an imprecise claim.
recommendation: Tighten wording in a follow-up doc pass to 'Sync-before-return durability discipline' rather than implying a literal shared WriteAt call.

summary: docs/LLD/wal.md remains scaffold-level and was not updated to document the concrete 8-byte CRC32 record format, wal-<N>.log naming convention, or the deferred-torn-tail-validation decision -- same staleness pattern already flagged for engine/btree in prior runs (032/034-verification), inherited not newly introduced.
recommendation: Do an LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/wal.md (record format, segment naming, rotation boundary semantics, deferred torn-tail scope) before or alongside 1.3.2.

summary: No dedicated test exercises the exact-boundary case (a record whose total size lands exactly at maxSegmentBytes, correctly kept in the current segment per code trace) or resuming with more than one pre-existing segment file.
recommendation: Add a follow-up test asserting a record landing exactly at the maxSegmentBytes boundary stays in the current segment, and a resume test with >1 pre-existing segment file.

summary: TestFsyncBeforeApply proves write-before-apply ordering and cache-coherent visibility (via a fresh os.ReadFile-based re-read inside apply()), not durability (fsync-before-apply) specifically -- it would still pass unchanged even if Writer.Append's underlying file.Sync() call were silently removed in a future regression, since same-process reads observe page-cache-resident writes regardless of fsync. Current implementation verified correct by direct code trace of writer.go's Append (Sync() genuinely called and checked before returning success); this is a future-regression-detection gap, not a live defect.
recommendation: Close via 1.3.5's crash-injection work (real process-kill-and-restart defeats OS page-cache coherency) or by refactoring Writer.file to an injectable Write+Sync interface enabling a spy-based unit test that asserts Sync() was called before apply() fires.

summary: RecordTypeInvalid=0 is documented as a reserved sentinel modeled on this codebase's fileID==0 (catalog.InvalidFileID) / nodeID==0 (btree.reservedNodeID) conventions, but unlike those (which have active runtime guards), DecodeTypedRecord performs no validation rejecting RecordTypeInvalid or any unrecognized RecordType byte -- a corrupted/garbage record with Type 0 or >4 decodes without error.
recommendation: Add a validation check in DecodeTypedRecord (or at 1.3.4's recovery-replay dispatch point) rejecting RecordTypeInvalid and out-of-range RecordType values, matching the InvalidFileID/reservedNodeID runtime-guard precedent.

summary: docs/LLD/wal.md remains scaffold-level and was not updated by this diff to document the RecordType vocabulary, payload encodings, or AppendAndApply's error semantics -- the LLD staleness gap flagged in 1.3.1's verification (036-verification) is now compounding across a second consecutive subtask.
recommendation: Do an LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/wal.md before or alongside 1.3.3, covering record types, payload encodings, and the fsync-before-apply contract.

summary: checkpoint.go's doc comment and the commit message claim the temp-file+Sync+os.Rename atomic-write idiom 'matches engine/btree/persist.go's SaveRoot precedent.' Disproven by direct read: SaveRoot uses in-place WriteAt+Sync with no temp file/rename, and a repo-wide grep finds zero other os.Rename call sites in engine/. Checkpoint's actual implementation is correct and genuinely atomic, but the stated architectural lineage is false.
recommendation: Correct checkpoint.go's doc comment to state this is a new atomic-write idiom for this codebase, not one mirroring SaveRoot's existing technique. Consider upgrading SaveRoot to the stronger idiom in a future subtask for consistency.

summary: Round-trip fidelity (Checkpoint then LoadCheckpoint) is only empirically exercised for one (segmentNumber, offsetInSegment) pair across the whole test suite, and no test covers calling Checkpoint twice in succession to confirm a clean overwrite rather than corruption of manifest.json.
recommendation: Add a table-driven round-trip test across several distinct pairs (including offset=0, a large segment number) plus a double-Checkpoint-overwrite test.

summary: docs/LLD/wal.md remains scaffold-level and was not updated to document the manifest.json schema or ArchivableSegments' strictly-less-than boundary semantics -- LLD staleness gap flagged in 1.3.1/1.3.2's verifications now compounds across a third consecutive subtask.
recommendation: Do an LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/wal.md covering the checkpoint pointer format, manifest.json's atomic-write contract, and ArchivableSegments' boundary rule, ideally before 1.3.4.

summary: Writer's public API (Append, SegmentNum, Close only) provides no supported way for a real caller to obtain 'current end-of-segment offset' (equivalently, 'offset after the last applied record'), which is exactly the value recovery.go's inclusive-offset checkpoint convention requires. recordHeaderSize is also unexported. 1.3.4's own tests only obtain this value via test-package conveniences (a subsequent record's Append return value, or os.Stat on a closed writer's segment file), neither available to a real live integration checkpointing mid-operation.
recommendation: Before 1.3.5 or any real catalog/btree integration calls Checkpoint() from live usage, add a public Writer.Offset()/Size() method returning the writer's current end-of-segment byte offset.

summary: No test in the wal package (1.3.1 through 1.3.4) constructs a deliberately CRC-corrupted (not type-invalid) mid-stream record and exercises the CRC-mismatch error path through ReadSegment or the new readSegmentFrom/Replay. Code is correct by inspection and errors are not swallowed, but the path is untested.
recommendation: Add a test (likely 1.3.5's crash-injection scope) that flips a payload byte in a written segment and asserts Replay returns a hard error rather than truncating or succeeding silently.

summary: readSegmentFrom (recovery.go) duplicates writer.go's ReadSegment parsing/CRC-check logic almost line-for-line rather than sharing a helper, per the commit's explicit rationale of not touching the already-verified writer.go. Reasonable short-term but a duplicated-logic maintenance liability.
recommendation: Consider a follow-up refactor where ReadSegment delegates to readSegmentFrom(path, 0), or both share an extracted parseRecordsFrom helper.

summary: docs/LLD/wal.md remains scaffold-level and was not updated to document Replay's contract, the OffsetInSegment inclusive-start convention, or the RecordType validation gate -- LLD staleness gap flagged across 1.3.1/1.3.2/1.3.3's verifications now compounds across a fourth consecutive subtask.
recommendation: Do an LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/wal.md covering Replay's semantics end-to-end before or alongside 1.3.5.

summary: Replay's guard for a checkpoint segment number greater than the last existing segment (on-disk-state-inconsistent-with-checkpoint scenario) is present and correct by code read but not exercised by any test.
recommendation: Add a test constructing a manifest.json pointing at a segment number beyond any existing wal-<N>.log file and asserting Replay returns the expected hard error.

summary: Closed all four deferred WAL crash-safety gaps: OpenWriter's resume path now validates+truncates a torn tail (or fails closed on real CRC corruption) via a new shared parseSegmentRecords helper also used by ReadSegment/readSegmentFrom/Replay; Replay tolerates a torn tail only in the last segment and now applies all records parsed before a CRC error rather than discarding them; added TestReplayCRCCorruption exercising a bad-CRC record end-to-end through Replay; added TestFsyncDurabilitySubprocessCrash (subprocess SIGKILL-based real-process-boundary durability test, with documented rigor-level limitation: proves no in-Go buffering, not physical-disk fsync durability); added Writer.Offset() int64 getter.

ContentStore.Create has no duplicate/already-exists guard: calling Create twice for the same fileID silently overwrites both content/<fileID>.v1.md (via writeContentFile's temp+rename) and the catalog record (Catalog.Put is an upsert). No test exercises this or empty/very-large content inputs.
recommendation: Add a test asserting the intended semantics of a duplicate Create on an existing fileID (either document it as legal overwrite, or add an already-exists guard), and add empty/large-content coverage, likely alongside 1.4.2-1.4.4's read/version/delete work.

summary: docs/LLD/catalog.md and docs/LLD/wal.md remain 'scaffold only' placeholders and were not updated to document the new ContentStore type/contract, continuing the LLD-staleness gap flagged across multiple prior subtask verifications.
recommendation: Do an LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/catalog.md covering ContentStore's create/write path and its WAL-before-apply wiring.

summary: ContentStore.Append performs an unsynchronized read-modify-write of the content file (no per-fileID lock, unlike Catalog's own striped-mutex design). Reproduced experimentally: 50 concurrent 1-byte Append() calls against the same fileID silently lost 49 of 50 appends (final content length 1, not 50); catalog SizeBytes matched the corrupted result with no error surfaced to any caller.
recommendation: Add a per-fileID lock around Append's read-append-write sequence (or route through Catalog's existing striped-mutex mechanism) before Append is exposed to any concurrent caller, and add a go test -race concurrent-Append test analogous to TestCatalogConcurrentDistinctFileIDs. If concurrency safety is intentionally deferred to Epic 2B alongside real split logic, document Append as single-writer-only in its doc comment.

summary: The 1.4.3 fix cycle (commit d10a468) correctly serializes ContentStore.Append per-fileID via an independent striped-mutex array, fixing the prior 49/50 lost-update bug (verified: 10/10 -race repro runs clean, full suite + count=3 pass, no regressions). Remaining low-risk gap: no test exercises concurrent Append vs. concurrent Read on the same fileID; the no-torn-read guarantee (write-temp-then-rename, content write ordered before cat.Put) is sound by inspection but not empirically pinned down by a -race test.
recommendation: Add a targeted -race test interleaving Append and Read goroutines on the same fileID to lock in the no-torn-read guarantee against future refactors of writeContentFile or the WAL-before-apply ordering.

summary: NewSnapshot (read.go) reads CurrentVersion via cat.Get() THEN acquires the current epoch via em.AcquireCurrentEpoch() as two separate, unsynchronized steps. If a concurrent CommitVersion completes fully in between (advancing the epoch and recording the new supersession epoch for the just-pinned version), the Snapshot ends up holding an epoch that is >= the epoch its own pinned version was superseded at, even though it is still pinned to the OLD version. RunCompaction (gc.go) then judges that old version reclaimable (MinReferencedEpoch >= supersededAtEpoch), deletes its file, and a subsequent snap.Read() on the still-open Snapshot fails with file-not-found. This violates the literal 2a.2.2 acceptance criteria (deleted only once its epoch refcount is zero) and the Snapshot doc comment guarantee that a pinned version file exists for the Snapshot lifetime. Reproduced deterministically in verification (temporary, non-committed test).
recommendation: Reorder NewSnapshot to call em.AcquireCurrentEpoch() before cat.Get(), so the acquired epoch is always <= the true epoch of the version read (never newer). Add a regression test using a synchronization hook (mirroring the existing afterWALBeforeApply/afterSnapshotBeforeRead pattern) that deterministically interleaves a concurrent CommitVersion between NewSnapshot internal steps and asserts RunCompaction never reclaims the pinned version. Correct the now-inaccurate race-note comment in read.go once fixed.

summary: TestGCUnderConcurrency (gc_test.go, commit d69cf0a) is a genuine broad concurrency stress test that satisfies its literal acceptance criteria and passes reliably (30+ -race runs, no flakiness, no regressions elsewhere). However, its commit message and doc comment claim it exercises 'the same class of race' as 2a.2.2's TestNewSnapshotClosesEpochAcquireVersionReadRace (the epoch-acquire-before-version-read TOCTOU). Empirically this is false: reverting read.go's NewSnapshot to the pre-2a.2.2-fix ordering (in an isolated, non-committed copy of the module) and re-running TestGCUnderConcurrency -race -count=10 then -count=20 (30 runs total) produced 30/30 PASSES -- zero detections -- while the deterministic hook-based regression test fails immediately (1/1) on the same reverted code. Additionally, instrumentation showed the 8 reader goroutines (bounded to 15 rounds each) all finish in ~107ms of the claimed 1500ms testDuration, so 'long-running readers... throughout' only actually overlaps writers/compactor for ~7% of the test's wall-clock window.
recommendation: Update TestGCUnderConcurrency's doc comment (gc_test.go lines ~515-529) to not overclaim equivalence to the deterministic TestNewSnapshotClosesEpochAcquireVersionReadRace regression test; state it is a complementary broad-stress test for general premature-reclaim bugs, not a substitute for the narrow deterministic test which remains the only reliable guard against that specific ordering bug reappearing. Optionally: have readers loop until the shared 'stop' channel fires (instead of a fixed readerRounds=15) so the actual overlap window matches the documented 'throughout the test duration' framing; optionally add a multi-fileID variant given EpochManager's epoch counter is global/store-wide rather than per-fileID.

summary: Tree.Insert's latch-crabbing implementation (commits eff41a0/edd81f9) satisfies its literal acceptance criteria at the scale exercised by the shipped TestCrabbingInsert (1800 keys, 30-40 goroutines): window-of-2 crabbing discipline, lock ordering, error-path Unlock coverage, rootMu scope, and LowKey immutability were all independently verified sound by code inspection. However, TestCrabbingInsert's key counts (1800 max) are far below the ~150-170 fanout^2 needed to force even one concurrent internal-node split with this NodeSize/key shape (confirmed empirically: the shipped test's final tree never exceeds 1 internal level, root NextSibling always 0), so the propagate 'ancestor overflowed, split it too' branch and findParent's internal-level move-right/retry-on-lost-race logic -- the subtask's central Blink-tree innovation and highest-risk code -- is never exercised by CI. Pushing to the depth-2+ regime (64 goroutines, 30000 keys, non-committed adversarial harness) reproduced a genuine intermittent (~1-in-20 to 1-in-25 runs) 'btree: internal invariant violated: findParent reached leaf N while searching for the current parent of M' failure: a real data-loss-class bug in concurrent internal-split propagation, not a test artifact (repro is deterministic-enough to hit reliably across repeated batches, root cause not yet bisected).
recommendation: Root-cause and fix the concurrent internal-split propagation bug (likely in findParent's or crabInsert's internal-level move-right/LowKey-peek logic, or an invariant violated by concurrent multi-level promotions -- see verification.json's confirmed_bugs entry for two candidate hypotheses). Once fixed, add a regression test at sufficient key-count/fanout to force >=2 internal levels with genuine concurrent internal splits (the current TestCrabbingInsert must be strengthened, e.g. raise perGoroutine/goroutines by an order of magnitude for the OverlappingSubtree case, or add a third subtest specifically targeting deep-tree concurrent internal splits), and extend assertStructuralInvariants to validate InternalNode.NextSibling chain consistency and LowKey boundary correctness at every internal level, not just leaf-level NextLeaf/sorted-keys. This is a blocking foundational bug for 2a.4.3 (delete) and 2a.4.5 (mixed workload), which will inherit findParent/propagate as-is.

summary: Resolution of the 020-verification regression above. Reproduced the exact reported failure first (own scratch 64-goroutine/~30,080-key harness, hit at attempt 3/40 and attempt 12/60 pre-fix, matching the verifier's error string exactly). Root cause was NEITHER of verification.json's two stated hypotheses: findParent's internal-level move-right/LowKey logic is correct, and there is no grandparent-promotion race invalidating routing. The actual gap: findParent's isLeaf branch had zero recovery when the landed-on leaf (still the ancestor's currently-registered, valid child) is not childID itself -- under real concurrency, chains of not-yet-linked leaf splits (e.g. leaf 36 -> 37 -> 38 via NextLeaf) commonly form faster than each split's own propagate call can link the new sibling into their shared ancestor, and childID can legitimately be a few NextLeaf hops past the ancestor's currently-registered child. Fix: findParent's isLeaf branch now walks the bounded NextLeaf chain looking for childID (mirroring crabInsert's own leaf-level move-right peek and findParent's existing NextSibling recovery loop), tracking the last internal ancestor visited (ancestorID) so it can still be returned as the correct parent; a defensive guard also rejects the (should-be-impossible) case of rootID itself decoding as a leaf. Re-ran the original repro 40/40 clean post-fix. Strengthened assertStructuralInvariants (per-internal-level NextSibling-chain-completeness + LowKey==subtree-min-key checks, made tolerant of transiently-empty leaves so pre-existing Delete tests are unaffected) and added TestCrabbingInsert/DeepOverlappingSubtree (64 goroutines, ~30,080 keys, explicit depth>=2 assertion) to close the CI-blindness gap from test_coverage_point6. Full engine/btree suite green (-race, count=1), zero regressions; TestCrabbingInsert -race -count=10 also clean (10/10).
recommendation: Re-verify via /cdr:verify: confirm the NextLeaf-chain-walk fix is sound (no missed edge case, e.g. childID split further ahead than the chain walked before this propagate call itself times out/retries elsewhere), confirm assertStructuralInvariants' new internal-level checks are themselves correct and do not mask other issues, and confirm the new DeepOverlappingSubtree subtest reliably reaches depth>=2 across repeated CI runs, not just this session's runs. This implementation-fix agent did not verify its own work per invariant I4.

summary: Re-verification of the 021-implementation-fix fix cycle (commits f0e972c/a07d732) for 020-verification's CHANGES_REQUESTED. The specific reported bug IS fixed: code-traced findParent's new bounded NextLeaf-chain-walk recovery and confirmed its ancestorID bookkeeping correctly matches what propagate's indexOfChild retry logic expects (manual race trace: leaf 36->37 split racing a second 37->38 split before propagate(36) links 37 into ancestor A, findParent(childID=37) correctly walks 36->37 and returns A, propagate correctly retries via existing lost-race path until A is actually linked). 40/40 clean re-runs of both the original 64g/30,080-key repro and the shipped TestCrabbingInsert/DeepOverlappingSubtree at -count=40; assertStructuralInvariants' new LowKey/NextSibling-chain checks are genuinely independent (ground truth via a separate recursive subtreeMinKey(), not tautological re-derivation). Full engine test suite green (-race, count=1), zero regressions. HOWEVER: per the re-verification task's explicit instruction to push harder than the original repro, a new temporary adversarial harness (160 goroutines, 80,000 keys, same striped key pattern, -race) found a DIFFERENT and more severe failure mode -- silent data loss (a previously-inserted key becomes unfindable via Lookup, no error surfaced by Insert or anywhere else) at ~8.6% (3/35 runs) -- not reproduced at the shipped test's 64g/30k scale across 40 repeated runs. This is a genuinely new bug exposed only by higher concurrency, not a recurrence of the just-fixed bug, and blocks the subtask from PASS.
recommendation: Do not close issue #9 yet. Root-cause the silent-data-loss failure at higher concurrency (160g/80k keys) before proceeding to 2a.4.3 (delete) or 2a.4.5 (mixed workload), which will inherit this code as-is. Candidate areas: propagate's ancestor-overflow chain across 3+ levels under sustained high concurrency, or a race in insertIntoLeafAndPropagate's own leaf split-and-link sequence at depths/scales beyond what any current test reaches. Add a committed regression test at this higher scale (or higher) once root-caused and fixed, since the current DeepOverlappingSubtree test (64g/30,080 keys) does not reach the concurrency level needed to reproduce it.

summary: Resolution of the 022-verification silent-data-loss regression above. Root-caused Tree.propagate inserting a promoted separator key at a stale positional index (j, the current index of the child that just split) rather than promotedKey's own sorted position, which allowed two children of the same parent to split and promote concurrently in an order that corrupts parent.Keys' sortedness -- once Keys is unsorted, sort.Search-based routing used by Lookup silently misroutes some already-inserted keys, which then become permanently unreachable with no error surfaced anywhere. Confirmed by code inspection (re-reading Tree.propagate's per-parent lock/retry loop and findParent's leaf-chain-walk in full) AND by a counterfactual revert-and-reproduce test: reverting to the pre-fix code and re-running the 160-goroutine/80,000-key repro harness reproduced the identical failure signature (unsorted internal node Keys + missing Lookup keys) at 3/15 (20%), consistent with 022-verification's ~8.6% (3/35) finding. Fix: sort.Search over parent.Keys for the true insertion position instead of the stale positional index. Also hardened the fix's own defensive 'pos < j should be unreachable' fallback, which originally silently fell back to the old buggy behavior if ever triggered -- changed to return a distinct invariant-violation error instead, so any future violation of that invariant fails loudly rather than silently regressing. Fix confirmed to eliminate the repro across 79 total clean runs (one intermediate 15-run batch showing 1/15 failures was traced to self-inflicted test contamination -- a concurrent git stash push/pop mutating insert.go mid-build while that batch's background loop was still running -- and was redone cleanly, sequentially, yielding 30/30 clean). Round-1 regression (TestCrabbingInsert/DeepOverlappingSubtree, 64g/30,080k) reconfirmed clean at -count=20 and -count=30. Added TestCrabbingInsert/VeryDeepOverlappingSubtree (160g/80k) as a new committed subtest, with its doc comment explicitly documenting the runtime-vs-reliability tradeoff: this scale only reproduces the bug at ~8.6% per run, so a single CI run is not a fully reliable single-shot regression guard for this specific race, but it is the smallest scale empirically observed to reproduce it at all. Full engine/btree suite and whole engine-module suite green (-race, count=1), zero regressions. Deleted the temporary zzrepro_test.go harness before commit.
recommendation: Re-verify via /cdr:verify: independently confirm the sort.Search-based insertion-position fix is sound against other interleavings not covered by this run's reasoning/testing (e.g. 3+ concurrent same-parent promotions, or promotions crossing multiple ancestor levels simultaneously), confirm the hardened defensive-fallback error path is itself correctly reachable/unreachable as claimed, and consider whether VeryDeepOverlappingSubtree's ~8.6%-per-run reproduction rate is an acceptable permanent CI tradeoff or whether a more targeted (non-probabilistic) unit-level test of propagate's insertion-position logic should be added. This implementation-fix agent did not verify its own work per invariant I4.

summary: Third verification pass on GitHub issue #9. Round-2 fix (d145f08/5a09c40) claims Tree.propagate's insertion-position formula (stale positional index j vs. sort.Search-derived sorted position) was the root cause of the ~8.6% silent-data-loss bug found in 022-verification. This pass found strong empirical support that the SYMPTOM is gone (75 additional independent clean runs across 3 scales -- 20x160g/80k, 40x160g/80k isolated, 15x320g/160k isolated -- plus the implementer's own 79 clean runs = 154 cumulative clean runs, vs. an original ~8.6% baseline), but found the stated MECHANISM does not hold up: a deterministic, exhaustive 24-permutation white-box test calling Tree.propagate directly for a 4-way same-parent concurrent split/promotion (the exact scenario illustrated in the fix's own doc comment) produces byte-identical, correct results under BOTH the new sort.Search-based formula AND a test-local reimplementation of the pre-fix plain positional-index (j) formula (source file never modified; confirmed via git diff). This is because j = indexOfChild(...) is always freshly computed under the parent's lock, and promotedKey is always mathematically bounded by parent.Keys[j-1] and parent.Keys[j] by construction, so pos == j is provably forced whenever the tree's own invariants hold -- meaning the two formulas are equivalent for the illustrated scenario, and the fix's apparent empirical success is not explained by its own stated causal narrative. The true root cause remains unidentified; plausible unexplored candidates are propagate's recursive 'ancestor overflowed, split it too' branch and a 3+-way concurrent not-yet-linked-leaf -chain scenario in findParent, neither of which this or the prior fix cycle's testing covered. Also flagged: the new 'pos < j' hard-error guard is mathematically unreachable in the illustrated scenario (consistent with its own 'should be unreachable' framing) but the symmetric 'pos > j' case is unguarded and could silently corrupt Children (not just Keys) if parent.Keys were ever corrupted by an unrelated bug. Build/vet/gofmt/full engine-module test suite all green; zzrepro_test.go confirmed deleted; no data races detected by -race across any run in this pass.
recommendation: Do not close issue #9 as fully root-caused. Either construct a genuine deterministic repro tied to a confirmed mechanism (e.g. via propagate's overflow-recursion branch, or a 3+-way not-yet-linked findParent scenario), or instrument a real failing run to capture actual j/pos/Keys values at the moment of corruption. The fix is very likely safe to keep given 154 cumulative clean runs and the fail-loud hardening being a net positive regardless of mechanism, but correct/soften propagate's doc comment (it currently asserts a causal claim disproved by this verification pass), guard the 'pos > j' case symmetrically with 'pos < j', and run VeryDeepOverlappingSubtree at -count>=10 in CI (or add a deterministic unit test) rather than relying on its ~8.6% single-run detection power as the sole regression guard for a silent-data-loss-class defect on its third verification pass.

resolves_run: orchestrator-caught (2026-07-06, not a subagent run): 43+ minute genuine hang of 021/023/024's TestCrabbingInsert/VeryDeepOverlappingSubtree (160g/80k) during round-3 implementation-fix, SIGQUIT-captured goroutine dump saved at scratchpad/goroutine_dump_deadlock.log; also very likely the same underlying defect behind 022-verification/024-verification's unconfirmed ~8.6% silent-data-loss finding
summary: Orchestrator (not a subagent) personally observed the round-3 stress test genuinely DEADLOCKED (not slow) for 40+ minutes, 96 CPU-minutes idle-spinning, before sending SIGQUIT and capturing a full goroutine dump. Dump showed multiple goroutines permanently blocked on sync.Mutex.Lock inside NodeStore.Lock, called from Tree.findParent (insert.go) and Tree.propagate, each waiting on a node latch held by ANOTHER blocked goroutine -- a genuine lock-ordering CYCLE, not merely a data race. Root cause: crabInsert/findParent's hand-over-hand ('lock next, release current') descent used a blocking Lock for every step; because a node in this tree design can be reached both via its parent's already-linked Children entry AND via a not-yet-linked sibling's NextLeaf/NextSibling pointer at the same time, two concurrent crabbing walks could each end up holding a latch the other needed, with no possible strict global lock-ordering to prevent it locally. Fix (structural, not a patch): added NodeStore.TryLock (latch.go); every hand-over-hand step in crabInsert/findParent now uses TryLock instead of Lock, and on a miss releases everything currently held and restarts the entire walk from the root (errRestartFromRoot, insert.go) after a small jittered backoff. This makes a lock-ordering cycle structurally impossible: no goroutine can ever be simultaneously blocked on a latch while holding a different latch someone else needs. Verified via: (1) new fast (<0.1s) deterministic regression test TestCrabbingConcurrentPropagateNoDeadlock using a synchronization hook (crabRetryHook, mirroring engine/mvcc's hook-based pause pattern) to force the exact TryLock-collision interleaving without large-scale random contention; (2) TestCrabbingInsert (including VeryDeepOverlappingSubtree, 160g/80k) plus the new test at -race -count=10 -timeout 5m: 165s, all pass, no hang; (3) full engine module at -race -count=1 -timeout 5m: all pass. Also re-confirmed (independently of 024-verification) that round 2's sort.Search fix was a no-op equivalent to the pre-fix 'pos := j' formula; reverted to the simpler form with an accurate comment. Does not independently re-prove the original silent-data-loss symptom is fixed by this specific change -- plausible given the shared mechanism (a path that would deadlock under heavy contention may instead silently misroute under lighter contention if some path swallows the condition instead of blocking forever), but not separately demonstrated; flagged for /cdr:verify.
recommendation: Verify: (a) the TryLock+restart-from-root mechanism is sound and covers every hand-over-hand lock acquisition site in crabInsert/findParent (propagate's own direct store.Lock(parentID) was deliberately left blocking since it never holds a second latch concurrently, so cannot itself participate in a cycle -- confirm this reasoning); (b) TestCrabbingConcurrentPropagateNoDeadlock genuinely exercises the fixed code path (not a tautological hook-only check); (c) re-run the original ~8.6% silent-data-loss repro conditions (160g/80k, multiple batches) to gather evidence on whether this fix also resolves that symptom, since it was never independently re-confirmed in this round.

summary: Round-3 fix (efa5cef/7c10bba) verified sound on 4th pass. TryLock-and-restart-from-root structurally eliminates the confirmed deadlock (30 additional clean repro runs of the 160g/80k harness, zero hangs, on top of implementer own 10 runs). Own 30-run repro attempt at same scale for the previously-unconfirmed silent-data-loss symptom (022-verification, 3/35 pre-fix) found zero instances post-fix -- favorable but not airtight (~7% false-negative chance) evidence both symptoms shared root cause and are resolved. No retry cap on restart loop: no data-loss-via-give-up risk, only a theoretical (never observed) livelock risk, standard/acceptable for this design. Full build/vet/gofmt/race test suite clean, no regressions elsewhere.
recommendation: Close subtask 2a.4.2 / GitHub issue #9. No further verification round required. Two low-risk open items carried forward as background context (no retry-attempt metric; data-loss repro not 100% mathematically proven absent) -- neither blocking.

title: Dangling cross-grandparent NextSibling after degenerate-ancestor splice is load-bearing, not inert
description: spliceOutDegenerateAncestor (engine/btree/delete.go) only patches a same-grandparent left neighbor NextSibling pointer (gj > 0 case) and treats the fix-up as best-effort/skippable. Whenever the spliced degenerate ancestor is its grandparents FIRST child (gj == 0), the true left neighbor lives under an adjacent grandparent and is deterministically never patched, leaving a permanently dangling NextSibling (node IDs are never reused/freed). This was claimed inert (never affects lookup/insert/delete correctness) but crabDeleteOnce and crabInsertOnce both actively dereference NextSibling as a move-right-on-overshoot mechanism during top-down descent (same as findParent), so a concurrent descent can be misrouted into an abandoned, Children-unreachable node.
recommendation: Handle gj==0 case (or make fix-up mandatory via errRestartFromRoot on miss), or add a descent-time safeguard that does not blindly trust a followed NextSibling link, plus a regression test constructing the gj==0 scenario.

finding: Header-offset cache invalidation is not atomic w.r.t. concurrent ReadPartial for the split paths (ExecuteSplitRedirectStub, ExecuteSplitAtomic): writeNewContentFile+cat.Put run without holding cs.stripes[stripeFor(originalFileID)], leaving a real window between durable cat.Put and InvalidateHeaderCache during which a concurrent ReadPartial with a pre-existing cache entry returns stale headers. Append path is correctly atomic (holds stripe lock for full critical section). TestSectionIndexInvalidation is fully serial (no goroutines) despite the acceptance criteria's -race requirement implying real concurrent exercise, so it does not catch this.

RESOLVED (fix cycle). Original entry at line referencing commit 4b8fe670 (subtask 2b.4.1, 2026-07-07T11:20:00Z, run_dir .cdr/runs/2026-07-07/030-verification) is now resolved=true. Both blocking findings fixed: ContentStore.LockFileContent(fileID) exports the same cs.stripes[stripeFor(fileID)] mutex Append/ReadPartial already use; ExecuteSplitRedirectStub and ExecuteSplitAtomic now hold it across their stub-content-write+cat.Put+InvalidateHeaderCache sequence, released only after (ExecuteSplitAtomic releases before B+Tree/graph-edge steps). New TestSectionIndexInvalidationConcurrent is a genuine -race concurrency test using a real goroutine + start barrier + atomicCommitHook test seam. Independently re-verified via revert-restore experiment: 10/10 -race failures with lock replaced by no-op, 10/10 -race passes restored. Full build/vet/gofmt/test matrix clean (split -race count=15 and count=1, catalog -race count=1, full engine ./... count=1).

summary: TestReaderDuringSplit uses a mvcc.VersionWriter root fully decoupled from the catalog.ContentStore root ExecuteSplitAtomic actually splits; mvcc.Snapshot.Read only consults CurrentVersion, which ExecuteSplitAtomic never mutates, so the test is a tautology and cannot detect a broken reader/split interaction despite running under -race with real goroutines.
recommendation: Rework the test to exercise a reader path that shares real state with the split (e.g. catalog.ContentStore.Read during StatusSplitting), or explicitly log the mvcc/ContentStore decoupling in .cdr/memory/pending.md and avoid claiming this issue fully closes the LLD mvcc-reader concurrency risk.

summary: Prior tautology gap genuinely fixed and independently reconfirmed (redid revert-experiment: 5/5 real torn-content catches; execute.go confirmed byte-for-byte unmodified). New, undisclosed finding: the test's 'reader observed post-split content at least once' assertion is sleep-timing-based (not a deterministic barrier) and produces an occasional FALSE FAILURE (~1-3% measured across ~265 runs, including one unprompted occurrence on a full go test ./... run under parallel-package load) when the split completes before the reader's loop overlaps it. This is a CI-reliability/flakiness concern, not a correctness-verification gap -- it never masks a real torn-read bug.
recommendation: Non-blocking fast-follow: replace the sleep-based overlap assumption in TestReaderDuringSplit with a deterministic synchronization barrier (e.g. signal via channel once ExecuteSplitAtomic's atomic rename occurs) to eliminate the false-failure flake before it becomes persistent CI noise on the epic's mandatory highest-risk gate.

summary: Fix cycle for prior F1 (retry-after-truncate-failure double-counts EdgeEntityCooccur weight, .cdr/runs/2026-07-08/008-verification) is genuinely correct and independently reproduced (worktree revert-experiment: new test fails at Weight=6 on pre-fix commit ebbc1ff, passes at Weight=3 on fix commit 9850083). However, the fix's own sidecar mechanism (graph.dat.compact-state, tracking per-node 'compacted-through segment number') introduces a new, more severe blocking bug F2: TruncateNode fully removes a node's edge-log directory, so wal.OpenWriter (wal/writer.go's latestSegmentNum) restarts segment numbering at 0 the next time that node is appended to. The sidecar's stale 'compacted-through segment N' entry from before truncation then causes the next Compact() to silently treat the brand-new reused segment number as already-reflected and skip it via ReadNodeAfter -- permanently and silently losing any edge appended to a node after its first successful truncation, on the very next ordinary (no-failure-injected) periodic-compaction pass. Independently reproduced with a minimal non-mocked repro: AppendEdge(weight=3) -> Compact (fully successful, no injection) -> AppendEdge(weight=5) -> Compact again -> LoadCSR shows Weight=3, not 3+5=8; edge log confirmed empty too, so the second edge exists nowhere.
recommendation: Do not key sidecar retry-skip logic on WAL segment numbers alone, since TruncateNode's remove-and-recreate-directory behavior makes them non-unique across a fileID's lifetime. Preferred fix: clear (delete) a node's sidecar entry as part of the same operation that successfully truncates its log, since a stale sidecar entry is only ever needed to bridge a FAILED truncation, not a successful one. Add a regression test for the normal 'truncate-then-append-then-compact-again' cycle (not just the failure-retry cycle) before re-verification.

description: task-3.1.2 (issue #15, verified PASS_WITH_COMMENTS per .cdr/commits/task-3.1.2.md, commits 32b8042/12eca06) has no entry in .cdr/index/task.jsonl, unlike 3.1.1/3.1.3/3.1.4/3.1.5. Non-blocking for 3.1.6 (test-only, doesn't touch index) but should be repaired before treating issue #15 tracking as fully consistent.

summary: proto/hivemind.proto's Neighbor.weight (line 103) is declared 'float weight = 3;' but the Go field it claims to mirror field-for-field, engine/graph.CSREdge.Weight (graph/csr.go:82), is 'uint32' -- an integer running SUM of edge occurrence counts (compact.go's mergeEdges), used for exact-equality tie-break sort comparisons in traverse.go. proto3 has a native 'uint32' scalar that would round-trip exactly; 'float' both mis-represents the field's integer/count semantics and risks silent precision loss for accumulated weights beyond float32's exact-integer range. Zero production Go/Python behavior changed otherwise; go build/go vet/gofmt/go mod verify all clean; full go test suite green except the known pre-existing TestReaderDuringSplit flake (baseline-matching, not a new regression); all other field-level cross-checks (ReadPartialResponse/HeaderOffset, ProposeSplitResponse/SplitFileProposal/SectionRange, EdgeType enum vs engine/graph/edge.go) correct; engine/split/engine/graph/engine/catalog confirmed untouched; provenance (final commit hash in metadata/handoff) confirmed correct.
recommendation: Change 'float weight = 3;' to 'uint32 weight = 3;' in proto/hivemind.proto and regenerate stubs before 3.2.2 (server.go) bakes a lossy/incorrect conversion into the wire path. Also non-blocking: resolve docs/LLD/rpc.md's 'Split' vs issue's 6-RPC-list naming-set ambiguity (disclosed judgment call by implementer) before 3.2.2 locks in the server's RPC surface.

summary: GetFile(FileId=0) and ReadPartial(FileId=0) return codes.Internal instead of a caller-input gRPC code (InvalidArgument/NotFound). Root cause: catalog.Catalog.Get special-cases FileID==0 (InvalidFileID) with a plain non-ErrNotFound error, which ContentStore.Read/ReadPartial propagate as a non-ErrNotFound fault, and mapCatalogError falls back to codes.Internal for anything that isn't wrapped ErrNotFound. FileId=0 is the proto3 zero-value for an unset field, a plausible ordinary client mistake, not an internal server fault. Untested edge case (existing NotFound subtests only use nonzero-nonexistent FileId=99999).
recommendation: Explicitly check for FileId==catalog.InvalidFileID (0) at the top of GetFile/ReadPartial (and any future handler resolving a bare file_id) and return codes.InvalidArgument before calling into catalog/content. Add a subtest for the zero-value case. Non-blocking; can be folded into 3.2.4 or a small standalone follow-up commit.

summary: RESOLVED. GetFile/ReadPartial now explicitly guard req.GetFileId()==catalog.InvalidFileID (0) at the top of each handler, returning codes.InvalidArgument before calling into ContentStore/Catalog, instead of falling through to mapCatalogErrors codes.Internal default. Regression subtests GetFile_ZeroFileID / ReadPartial_ZeroFileID added to engine/rpc/server_test.go asserting codes.InvalidArgument.

TestRPCIntegration is technically excellent (real net.Listener + real grpc.Server + real LatencyInterceptor wiring, confirmed genuinely novel vs bufconn-based prior tests; real backing catalog/content/btree/graph state cross-checked directly; correctly avoids known Neighbor.hop=0 and searchCandidateScore placeholder pitfalls; correctly asserts codes.Unimplemented for ProposeSplit via status.FromError). However issue #16's own written subtask 3.2.5 acceptance criteria require a real Go-engine <-> running-Python-agent-service round trip that successfully executes ProposeSplit and PutSegment -- no such cross-language test exists anywhere in the 3.2.x sequence. The delivered test is Go-only and explicitly expects ProposeSplit to fail with Unimplemented. This scope reduction was disclosed and consistently applied starting at 3.2.1's fix cycle (docs/LLD/rpc.md Status note: real Python-backed ProposeSplit server descoped to issue #18) and re-affirmed through 3.2.2/3.2.3/3.2.4's independent verifications, so it is not a new defect introduced by this commit -- but it means issue #16, read literally, is not fully satisfied by the 5 subtasks as implemented.
recommendation: Before closing issue #16: either (a) get explicit user/maintainer sign-off to accept the ProposeSplit-server-to-issue-#18 scope split and amend issue #16's 3.2.5 text/acceptance-criteria to match what was actually built, or (b) open/confirm issue #18 explicitly covers the real cross-language Go<->Python ProposeSplit+PutSegment round trip test so the originally-promised coverage is not silently dropped.

description: PAGE_MARKER_RE non-greedy capture silently truncates page content when a page text contains a literal substring matching its own close marker (e.g. literal [[/PAGE 1]] inside page 1 text). Undetected by marker-count-parity test since match count is unaffected, only content is corrupted. Confirmed via direct reproduction script.
recommendation: Sanitize/escape marker-like substrings in extracted text, or use a collision-resistant marker format (nonce-based or length-prefixed framing); add regression test before downstream segmentation/dispatch code depends on this marker format.

summary: RESOLVED. Replaced non-greedy backreference-based [[PAGE n]]...[[/PAGE n]] parsing with a length-prefixed marker format ([[PAGE n LEN=k]]...[[/PAGE n]]) plus a new iter_pages() parser that slices exactly k characters instead of scanning page content for a closing marker. Eliminates the entire delimiter-in-payload collision class (not just the self-referential reproduction case), so a page's own text may contain literal marker-lookalike substrings (its own close marker or another page's markers) without truncation. Reproduced F1 first via a throwaway script confirming silent truncation under the old regex; confirmed fixed after the change. Added regression tests test_page_text_containing_its_own_close_marker_survives_round_trip and test_page_text_containing_other_pages_marker_lookalike_survives, plus cheap F2 coverage (multi-digit page numbers, nonexistent-path and corrupt-file negative tests).

description: iter_pages() trusts the LEN=k header value verbatim with no independent boundary validation. If LEN is ever wrong (not reachable via current normalize_pdf/iter_pages code path, but possible if a future intermediate transformation touches the normalized text before parsing) and the resulting payload_end coincidentally lands on a same-page-number close-marker-lookalike substring inside the real payload, iter_pages() silently yields truncated content with no exception -- a narrower recurrence of the F1 content-dropping risk class. Confirmed via hand-constructed scratch script during re-verification attempt 2.
recommendation: Document the trust boundary in iter_pages()'s docstring, and/or add defense-in-depth validation before 3.3.4 (dispatch.py) introduces any storage/transport layer between normalize_pdf() and iter_pages().

description: No test exercises non-ASCII/multi-byte text (accented characters, CJK, emoji/astral-plane code points) through normalize_pdf/_page_marker/iter_pages. Hand-verified correct during re-verification attempt 2 (Python str operations are code-point-based end-to-end, no byte/char mismatch), but untested in CI.
recommendation: Add a regression test asserting iter_pages(_page_marker(1, unicode_text)) round-trips accented/CJK/emoji text intact.

finding: Shipped test fixtures for normalize_email.py do not cover multipart bodies, quoted-printable/Content-Transfer-Encoding, display-name From headers, multi-address Cc/Bcc lists, or folded headers -- code independently verified to handle these correctly, but behavior is unpinned by tests and could regress silently on future refactor.
